definition: A design smell where two or more class hierarchies mirror each other so that every time you add a subclass in one hierarchy you must add a corresponding subclass in the other hierarchy. This creates duplicated structure and tight coupling between the hierarchies, making evolution error-prone and costly.
ruleOfThumb: If you find yourself adding matching subclasses in multiple hierarchies, stop: consider combining responsibilities through composition or extracting the varying behavior into a single set of collaborating types.

citation.author: Martin Fowler
citation.bookTitle: Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code

questions.prompt: Look at the code. What problem is this most clearly showing?

options.text: Two separate hierarchies mirror each other so adding a new subtype requires changes in both places.
options.explanation: Correct. The Animal and AnimalSerializer hierarchies are duplicated: every new Animal leads to a matching Serializer. That symmetry signals coupled hierarchies that will force parallel edits.

options.text: A large class with too many responsibilities that should be split into smaller classes.
options.explanation: Incorrect. There isn't a single bloated class; responsibilities are split across classes but the issue is duplication across two hierarchies rather than one class doing everything.

options.text: Excessive use of global state making methods hard to test.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The snippet shows no global state. The problem is structural duplication across hierarchies, not test-unfriendly globals.

options.text: Missing interfaces—concrete classes should implement interfaces to allow swapping implementations.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The code already uses abstract base types. The core issue is the parallel, mirrored subclassing pattern, not a lack of abstraction.

questions.prompt: You need to make it easier to add new Animal types and new serialization formats independently. What is the best immediate refactor?

options.text: Merge each Animal subclass with its serializer into a single class so there are fewer types to maintain.
options.explanation: Wrong choice. Merging mixes responsibilities (behavior + serialization) into one class. That reduces duplication but increases coupling and violates single-responsibility; it makes adding new serializers harder.

options.text: Introduce a serializer/strategy interface and inject a serializer instance into animals (use delegation instead of parallel subclassing).
options.explanation: Right. Extracting serialization as a separate strategy and composing it with Animal decouples the two axes of variation. You can add new animals or serializers independently without creating matching subclasses.

options.text: Use mixins or multiple inheritance so each serializer can be combined with different animals at runtime.
options.explanation: Not ideal. Mixins increase complexity and can introduce brittle coupling and diamond problem–like behavior in TypeScript. Delegation via a small interface is simpler and clearer.

options.text: Keep the hierarchies and centralize the decision with a big factory that maps animals to serializers.
options.explanation: This postpones the coupling. A central factory still requires updating whenever you add a new Animal or serializer and can become a maintenance bottleneck; it doesn't decouple the two dimensions.

questions.prompt: Which design is the better long-term choice for maintainability and why? (Version A vs Version B)

options.text: Version A: Keep the mirrored hierarchies and centralize logic in a factory with instanceof checks.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The factory with instanceof checks preserves coupling: every new Animal requires editing the factory. That scatters knowledge about types and violates the open/closed principle; tests tend to grow brittle and duplication remains.

options.text: Version B: Inject serializer strategy instances into animal objects so animals and serializers can evolve independently.
options.explanation: Correct. Composition separates the two axes of change: you can add new serializers without touching animal subclasses and vice versa. This reduces duplicated subclassing, improves testability (you can inject mocks), and aligns with delegation and single-responsibility principles—making maintenance and extension safer.
